Hi support,

input.system:
You extract structured data from unstructured text. Respond with ONLY a JSON object containing exactly these fields:
- `invoice_number` (string): the invoice number, or null if not present
- `vendor` (string): the vendor name, or null if not present
- `customer_name` (string): the billed customer's name, or null if not present
- `invoice_date` (string): the invoice date in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not present
- `due_date` (string): the payment due date in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not present
- `line_items` (array of objects): each line item as an object with keys `description` (string), `quantity` (integer), `unit_price` (number), and `line_total` (number), or null if no line items present
- `subtotal` (number): the subtotal in pounds, or null if not present
- `discount` (number): the absolute discount amount applied in pounds (positive number), or null if no discount
- `total` (number): the final total in pounds, or null if not present

Do not include any other text.
input.user:
Acme Co Ltd — Invoice

Invoice number: INV-2026-1102
Issued: 5 May 2026
Terms: payment due within 21 days of issue

Bill to: Customer P

  Description                        Qty   Unit     Line
  Onboarding workshop                1     £800     £800
  Consulting hours                   10    £120     £1,200
  Custom integration build           1     £2,500   £2,500

Subtotal: £4,500
Loyalty discount (10%): -£450
Total due: £4,050

Please pay by bank transfer.

metadata.notes: Hard invoice extraction with a list of line items as nested objects, a due date computed from issue date plus 21 days, and a discount expressed as a negative line that must be normalised to a positive absolute number.

input.system:
You extract structured data from unstructured text. Respond with ONLY a JSON object containing exactly these fields:
- `lead_name` (string): the prospective customer's name, or null if not present
- `company` (string): the company name, or null if not present
- `email` (string): the best email address to reach them on, or null if not present
- `phone` (string): the best phone number in the exact format given, or null if not present
- `country` (string): the country they are based in, expressed as the full English country name (e.g. 'United Kingdom', not 'UK' or 'GB'), or null if not present
- `team_size` (integer): the size of their team, or null if not present
- `current_tooling` (array of strings): the tools they currently use, or null if not stated
- `requested_demo_date` (string): the requested demo date in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not present
- `budget_gbp` (number): the upper bound of their stated budget in pounds, or null if not present

Do not include any other text.
input.user:
Sent on Wednesday 6 May 2026 at 09:14

Hello,

I'm Customer Q, head of operations at Acme Co. We're a team of about 35 (UK based). I previously emailed from my personal account customer.q@example.org but please use my work email customer.q@acme.co going forward — the personal one is just for backup.

We currently use ToolExampleA for ticketing, ToolExampleB for analytics, and a homegrown spreadsheet for everything else. We'd love a demo — could we do next Tuesday? Our budget for this year is somewhere between £20k and £30k.

You can also reach me on +44 7700 900222.

Thanks,
Customer Q

metadata.notes: Hard lead-enrichment with multiple traps: two candidate emails where the explicitly preferred one wins, 'next Tuesday' resolved against a stated send date of Wed 6 May 2026, country inferred from 'UK based', a tooling list that excludes the homegrown spreadsheet, and a budget range whose upper bound is the requested value.

- `incident_id` (string): the incident reference, or null if not present
- `severity` (string): the severity level (e.g. 'P1', 'P2', 'P3'), or null if not present
- `start_time_utc` (string): the incident start time in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ, or null if not present
- `end_time_utc` (string): the incident end time in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ, or null if not present
- `duration_minutes` (integer): the total incident duration in whole minutes, or null if not derivable
- `affected_services` (array of strings): the list of affected services, or null if not stated
- `on_call_engineer` (string): the on-call engineer's name, or null if not present
- `customer_facing` (boolean): true if customers were impacted, false if not, null if not stated

Do not include any other text.
input.user:
Incident handover note (recorded 28 April 2026)

INC-20260428-04 — sev P2.

Started at 14:05 BST on Tue 28 April, recovered at 15:35 BST same day. (BST is UTC+1.)

Services hit: API gateway and the billing webhook processor. The mobile app stayed up.

On-call was Customer R. There were customer reports in the support inbox so this is definitely customer-facing.

Root cause TBD — handing over to the next on-call shift.

metadata.notes: Hard internal handover note; tests BST-to-UTC timezone conversion using the explicitly stated offset, derived duration in minutes, list extraction excluding an explicitly unaffected service, and boolean inference from indirect language.

input.system:
You extract structured data from unstructured text. Respond with ONLY a JSON object containing exactly these fields:
- `policy_name` (string): the name of the policy, or null if not present
- `version` (string): the policy version, or null if not present
- `effective_date` (string): the effective date in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not present
- `review_date` (string): the next review date in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not present
- `owner` (string): the policy owner's name, or null if not present
- `applies_to_permanent_employees` (boolean): true if the policy applies to permanent employees, false if not
- `applies_to_fixed_term_contractors_six_months_plus` (boolean): true if the policy applies to fixed-term contractors on six-month-plus contracts, false if not
- `applies_to_short_term_agency_workers` (boolean): true if the policy applies to short-term agency workers, false if not
- `max_consecutive_remote_days` (integer): the maximum number of consecutive remote-working days permitted, or null if not stated
- `requires_manager_approval` (boolean): true if manager approval is required for remote working, false if not, null if not stated

Do not include any other text.
input.user:
Acme Co — Remote Working Policy (v3.1)

This policy is owned by Customer S, Head of People. It comes into effect on the first of June 2026 and will be reviewed twelve months after that date.

The policy applies to permanent employees and to fixed-term contractors on contracts of six months or more. It does not apply to short-term agency workers.

Employees may work remotely for up to 10 consecutive working days at a time. Any request to work remotely must be approved in advance by the employee's line manager.

metadata.notes: Hard policy-document extraction; tests parsing 'the first of June 2026' and 'twelve months after that date' to ISO dates, and answering booleans per staff group. Restructured from free-text array to boolean-per-category to make rubric scoring deterministic.

- `role_applied_for` (string): the role title, or null if not present
- `years_experience` (integer): the applicant's total years of relevant experience as an integer, or null if not derivable
- `current_employer` (string): the applicant's current employer, or null if not stated
- `notice_period_weeks` (integer): the notice period in whole weeks, or null if not stated
- `desired_salary_gbp` (number): the desired annual salary in pounds, or null if not stated

input.user:
Application — Senior Data Engineer

Name: Customer T
Contact: customer.t@example.com

I'm currently a Lead Data Engineer at Acme Co, where I've been for the last three years. Before that I spent five years at a fintech in London as a Data Engineer. I'd be looking for a salary of £85,000 and I'm on a two-month notice. I have unrestricted right to work in the UK.

Key skills: Python, SQL, Airflow, dbt, AWS.

Looking forward to hearing from you.

metadata.notes: Hard job-application extraction; tests summing 3+5 years of experience, converting 'two-month notice' to 8 weeks, and parsing a comma-separated skills list with mixed casing preserved.

input.system:
You extract structured data from unstructured text. Respond with ONLY a JSON object containing exactly these fields:
- `customer_name` (string): the customer's name, or null if not present
- `account_id` (string): the account identifier, or null if not present
- `email` (string): the email address, or null if not present
- `complaint_summary` (string): a one-sentence summary of the complaint in your own words, or null if no complaint stated
- `requested_action` (string): one of 'refund', 'replacement', 'callback', 'cancellation', 'other', or null if not stated
- `incident_date` (string): the date of the incident in ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DD, or null if not derivable
- `order_value_gbp` (number): the disputed order value in pounds, or null if not stated
- `previous_ticket_ids` (array of strings): the list of previously raised ticket IDs referenced, or null if none

Do not include any other text.
input.user:
Email received on Friday 24 April 2026.

Hi,

This is Customer U (account ACC-3320, customer.u@example.com). I am writing — again — about the order I placed three weeks before this email was sent, which arrived damaged. I have already raised tickets TCK-9001 and TCK-9014 and have heard nothing useful from either.

The order was £312.50. At this point I do not want a replacement; I would like a full refund processed to the original card.

If I do not hear back within five working days I will be raising a chargeback.

Regards,
Customer U

expected.complaint_summary: Customer U received a damaged order and has had no useful response to two prior tickets.

metadata.notes: Hard customer message; tests inferring an incident date as 'three weeks before' a stated send date of Fri 24 April 2026, picking 'refund' over the explicitly rejected 'replacement', and extracting a list of previously-raised ticket IDs. The complaint_summary field is free-form so tier-1 scoring will treat it loosely or skip it depending on rubric implementation.
